fields.definition_from_readme: Status designation, see more details above

value_definitions.name: Announced
value_definitions.definition: Projects that have appeared in corporate or governmental planning documents but have not yet moved actively forward by applying for permits or seeking land, coal, or financing.

value_definitions.name: Pre-permit
value_definitions.definition: Projects that have actively moved forward in one or more of the following ways: applying for environmental permits, acquiring land, acquiring coal, acquiring water rights, acquiring transmission arrangements, or securing financing. In India the key criterion for moving a project from "announced" to "pre-permit" is the existence of a "terms of reference" (TOR) letter by the Ministry of Environment, Forests, and Climate Change. In China, "pre-permit" means a feasibility study has been completed.

value_definitions.name: Permitted
value_definitions.definition: Projects that have secured all environmental permits but have not broken ground. In India the key criterion for moving a project from "pre-permit" to "permitted" is the approval of an "environmental clearance" (EC) by the Ministry of Environment, Forests, and Climate Change. In China, "permitted" means a plant has received a permit from the National Development and Reform Commission (NDRC), allowing for construction, or the equivalent approval at the province level.

value_definitions.name: Construction
value_definitions.definition: Projects where physical construction (i.e. concrete and steel, not just a ground-breaking ceremony or early site preparation) has begun.

value_definitions.name: Shelved
value_definitions.definition: Projects where sufficient evidence is found to indicate that a project is no longer moving forward, but not enough to declare it definitively cancelled. Projects where construction has been put on hold are also considered "shelved." A project that shows no activity over a period of 2 years is categorized as "shelved" unless there is evidence to the contrary.

value_definitions.name: Cancelled
value_definitions.definition: In some cases a sponsor announces that it has cancelled a project. More often a project fails to advance and then quietly disappears from related documents. In addition, a plant that shows no activity or with no known developments over a period of 4 years is categorized as "cancelled" (or two years since being marked "shelved"), unless there is evidence to the contrary. Proposed projects that are switched to natural gas are considered "cancelled" as coal plants.

value_definitions.name: Operating
value_definitions.definition: Units that have been formally commissioned or that have entered commercial operation.

value_definitions.name: Mothballed
value_definitions.definition: Units that have been deactivated or put into an inactive state but are not retired.

value_definitions.name: Retired
value_definitions.definition: Units that have been permanently decommissioned or converted to another fuel.

api_metadata.name: Status
api_metadata.definition: Status designation, see more details above

values_definitions.Announced: Projects that have appeared in corporate or governmental planning documents but have not yet moved actively forward by applying for permits or seeking land, coal, or financing.
values_definitions.Pre-permit: Projects that have actively moved forward in one or more of the following ways: applying for environmental permits, acquiring land, acquiring coal, acquiring water rights, acquiring transmission arrangements, or securing financing. In India the key criterion for moving a project from "announced" to "pre-permit" is the existence of a "terms of reference" (TOR) letter by the Ministry of Environment, Forests, and Climate Change. In China, "pre-permit" means a feasibility study has been completed.
values_definitions.Permitted: Projects that have secured all environmental permits but have not broken ground. In India the key criterion for moving a project from "pre-permit" to "permitted" is the approval of an "environmental clearance" (EC) by the Ministry of Environment, Forests, and Climate Change. In China, "permitted" means a plant has received a permit from the National Development and Reform Commission (NDRC), allowing for construction, or the equivalent approval at the province level.
values_definitions.Construction: Projects where physical construction (i.e. concrete and steel, not just a ground-breaking ceremony or early site preparation) has begun.
values_definitions.Shelved: Projects where sufficient evidence is found to indicate that a project is no longer moving forward, but not enough to declare it definitively cancelled. Projects where construction has been put on hold are also considered "shelved." A project that shows no activity over a period of 2 years is categorized as "shelved" unless there is evidence to the contrary.
values_definitions.Cancelled: In some cases a sponsor announces that it has cancelled a project. More often a project fails to advance and then quietly disappears from related documents. In addition, a plant that shows no activity or with no known developments over a period of 4 years is categorized as "cancelled" (or two years since being marked "shelved"), unless there is evidence to the contrary. Proposed projects that are switched to natural gas are considered "cancelled" as coal plants.
values_definitions.Operating: Units that have been formally commissioned or that have entered commercial operation.
values_definitions.Mothballed: Units that have been deactivated or put into an inactive state but are not retired.
values_definitions.Retired: Units that have been permanently decommissioned or converted to another fuel.

fields.definition_from_readme: Average capacity factor by country, based on GEM's global operating coal power capacity (2025) and Ember's power generation data (accessed 23 January 2026). Plants that are retired or cancelled are given a capacity factor of 0, i.e. no generation is assumed. Capacity data for Indonesia includes grid plants only, as the generation data excludes captive plants. Because the GCPT excludes coal-fired units under 30 MW, the average capacity factor reported here likely overstates national averages in countries with a non-trivial share of small units.

value_definitions.name: Plant age
value_definitions.definition: The age of the plant. For operating and mothballed plants it is the number of years since the plant began operating. For retired plants it is the age that the plant retired. Age is set to 0 for proposed plants and for plants that have been operating less than a year.

value_definitions.name: Lifetime
value_definitions.definition: Lifetime emissions assume a plant lifetime of 35 years, based on the global average retirement age of 37 years. Plants older than 35 years are assumed to operate an additional 5 years. Plants with a planned retirement date are assumed to cease operations that year.

value_definitions.name: Heat rate
value_definitions.definition: Heat rate is based on type of combustion technology, with performance penalties for older and smaller units. More discussion at https://www.gem.wiki/Estimating_carbon_dioxide_emissions_from_coal_plants

value_definitions.name: Emission factor
value_definitions.definition: Based on type of coal using the 2006 IPPC Guidelines for National Greenhouse Inventories, Table 2.2, page 2.16.

api_metadata.name: Capacity factor
api_metadata.definition: Average capacity factor by country, based on GEM's global operating coal power capacity (2025) and Ember's power generation data (accessed 23 January 2026). Plants that are retired or cancelled are given a capacity factor of 0, i.e. no generation is assumed. Capacity data for Indonesia includes grid plants only, as the generation data excludes captive plants. Because the GCPT excludes coal-fired units under 30 MW, the average capacity factor reported here likely overstates national averages in countries with a non-trivial share of small units.

api_metadata.name: Plant age (years)
api_metadata.definition: The age of the plant. For operating and mothballed plants it is the number of years since the plant began operating. For retired plants it is the age that the plant retired. Age is set to 0 for proposed plants and for plants that have been operating less than a year.

api_metadata.name: Heat rate (Btu per kWh)
api_metadata.definition: Heat rate is based on type of combustion technology, with performance penalties for older and smaller units. More discussion at https://www.gem.wiki/Estimating_carbon_dioxide_emissions_from_coal_plants

fields.field_name: Emission factor (kg of CO2 per TJ)

api_metadata.name: Emission factor (kg of CO2 per TJ)
api_metadata.definition: Based on type of coal using the 2006 IPPC Guidelines for National Greenhouse Inventories, Table 2.2, page 2.16.

api_metadata.name: Annual CO2 (million tonnes / annum)
api_metadata.definition: For CO2 calculation method, see "Estimating carbon dioxide emissions from coal plants" at https://www.gem.wiki/Estimating_carbon_dioxide_emissions_from_coal_plants

api_metadata.name: Remaining plant lifetime (years)
api_metadata.definition: Lifetime emissions assume a plant lifetime of 35 years, based on the global average retirement age of 37 years. Plants older than 35 years are assumed to operate an additional 5 years. Plants with a planned retirement date are assumed to cease operations that year.

fields.definition_from_readme: Whether the unit is eligible to receive payments under China's coal capacity payment mechanism. For more info see https://www.gem.wiki/China_Coal_Capacity_Payment_Mechanism

value_definitions.name: Metholology
value_definitions.definition: For CO2 calculation method, see "Estimating carbon dioxide emissions from coal plants" at https://www.gem.wiki/Estimating_carbon_dioxide_emissions_from_coal_plants

value_definitions.name: Note
value_definitions.definition: The GCPT estimates for CO2 emissions are intended to provide guidance on overall country and regional emissions over the lifecycle of the existing and proposed fleet of plants. They are not intended to provide detailed impact analysis for particular plants. For this reason, the average capacity factor by country is used for all plants. In general, newer plants will tend to have higher capacity factors than the average, and older plants will have lower capacity factors than the average. Similarly, heat rates and emission factors represent broad performance; these factors will differ for particular plants and coal supplies.

api_metadata.name: China capacity payment recipient
api_metadata.definition: Whether the unit is eligible to receive payments under China's coal capacity payment mechanism. For more info see https://www.gem.wiki/China_Coal_Capacity_Payment_Mechanism
